CFDocument.creator: Georgia Department of Education
CFDocument.title: World Languages
CFDocument.description:
On January 17, 2019, the State Board of Education approved 7 new Georgia Standards of Excellence (GSE) courses for world languages. These courses will be implemented during the 2019-2020 school year.

The courses include 6 new Middle School Dual Language Immersion Courses and a Spanish for Native Speakers Level 3 course.

Students develop an awareness of perspectives, practices, and products of the cultures where the target language is used.
Demonstrate knowledge of contributions target culture to civilization (e.g., technology; art; theater and plays; sign language/deaf literature, media, games and jewelry).
Identify commonly held viewpoints of the culture (e.g., cultural vs. medical views of deafness, D/d deaf, residential vs. inclusive models of education).
Describe customs and traditions of the culture, such as greetings, celebrations, and courtesies (e.g., name signs, homecoming, deaf/hearing/hard of hearing, deaf only organizations).
III. Connections, Comparisons, and Communities (CCC)
Connections, Comparisons, and Communities (CCC)
Students use information acquired in the study of the target language and information acquired in other subject areas to reinforce one another.

Demonstrate knowledge of the evolution and spread of American Sign Language (e.g., travels of T.H. Gallaudet; location of residential schools; Martha’s Vineyard; Gallaudet University/NTID/CSUN; regional signs).

Apply previously learned skills from other subjects, when appropriate to demonstrate knowledge in the target language (e.g., practice math fact drills; English/ASL grammar; famous deaf people; receptive fingerspelling).

Relate content from other subject areas to topics discussed in the language class (e.g., literature by or for deaf people; appearance of deaf characters in TV drama; sports; sign for and from other countries).
Students demonstrate an understanding of the significance of culture through comparisons between the culture studied and the students’ own culture.

Compare patterns of behavior and interaction in the students’ own culture with those of the target culture (e.g., phone/TV access; ring signalers, visual noise; proxemics; eye contact; driving; use of interpreters).
Demonstrate an awareness of elements of the students’ own culture (e.g., communication and social interaction; other languages in the home, extended family).
Students compare basic elements of the target language to the English language.
Recognize similarities and differences in English and the target language regarding idioms, slang (e.g., simple translations to /from English and ASL).
Recognize basic sign parameters and inflectional patterns (e.g., same/different).
Students demonstrate an awareness of current events in the target culture.
Give information about current events of target cultures (e.g., Deaf President Now, oral vs. manual).
Understand the impact of current events of the target culture (e.g., outcomes of Deaf President Now, No Child Left Behind, FCC-Video Phone, Americans with Disabilities Act, Miss Deaf America).

Students identify situations and resources in which the target language skills and cultural knowledge may be applied beyond the classroom setting for recreational, educational, and occupational purposes.
Identify examples of the target language and the culture studied that are evident in and through media, entertainment, and technology (Vlogs, ASL movies, careers requiring sign language skill).

Identify resources, such as individuals and organizations accessible through the community or the Internet, that provide basic cultural information about the culture studied.  (e.g., National Association of the Deaf, Registry of Interpreters for the Deaf, National Black Deaf Advocates, ASL web browser, Deaf Nation, local events).
I. Communication
Interpersonal Mode of Communication (IP)
Students exchange information in the target language, utilizing cultural references where appropriate.
Express needs and preferences (e.g., compare/contrast).
Express feelings and emotions (e.g., varying degrees of intensity, inflection).
Request help and clarification (e.g., how to communicate at a Deaf event).
Give descriptions (e.g., advanced classifiers).
Give and follow directions and instructions (e.g., locatives, and indexing).
Ask questions and provide responses based on topics such as self, others, and immediate environment.
Ask questions and provide responses about plans and events (e.g., time markers, listing, and sequencing).
Students demonstrate skills necessary to initiate, sustain and close exchanges in the target language.
Initiate, participate in, and close an exchange.
Use simple paraphrasing to convey and comprehend messages (e.g., retelling, and create story from picture stimuli).
Use non-manual aspects of ASL to convey and comprehend messages (e.g., eye gaze, and role shifting).
Demonstrate SLPI Survival to Survival+ or ASLPI Level 1.0 to 2.0 Proficiency in target language exchanges.
Interpretive Mode of Communication (INT)
Students understand information on new and familiar topics presented in the target language through a variety of media including authentic narratives and materials.
Identify main ideas and essential details while viewing an ASL signed narrative.
Comprehend culturally authentic ASL genres (e.g., poetry, jokes, and stories).
Understand instructions (e.g., 2-3 step directions, recipes).
Demonstrate SLPI Survival to Survival+ or ASLPI Level 1.0 to 2.0 Proficiency receptive skills.
Students will comprehend signed and non-manual markers to understand messages in the target language.
Differentiate among statements, questions, and commands (e.g., reciprocal verbs, distributional verbs).
Comprehend basic non-manual markers (e.g., facial grammar, mouth phonemes/morphemes).
Presentational Mode of Communication (P)
Students present information using newly acquired vocabulary, phrases, and patterns.
Retell main ideas and essential details from level-appropriate ASL materials (e.g., summarizing signed narratives).
Give brief, organized signed presentations, using visual and technological support as appropriate.
Demonstrate SLPI Survival to Survival+ or ASLPI Level 1.0 to 2.0 Proficiency expressive skills.
Students present rehearsed and unrehearsed material in the target language, such as dialogues, skits, and poetry.
Demonstrate SLPI Survival to Survival+ or ASLPI Level 1.0 to 2.0 Proficiency sign production skills of rehearsed and unrehearsed materials.
Demonstrate comprehension of material.
II. Cultural Perspectives, Practices, and Products (CU)
Cultural Perspectives, Practices, and Products
Students understand perspectives, practices, and products of the culture where the target language is used and how they are interrelated.
Participate in real or simulated cultural events (e.g., sign-only meals and activities).
Identify patterns of behavior typically associated with Deaf culture  (e.g., Deaf clubs, Deaf sports).
Examine international Deaf communities (e.g., foreign deaf communities and sign languages, international organizations, Deaf Way, World Federation of the Deaf, and Deaf Olympics).
III. Connections, Comparisons, and Communities (CCC)
Connections, Comparisons, and Communities (CCC)
Students use information acquired in the study of the target language and information acquired in other subject areas to reinforce one another.
Give examples of historical perspectives of American Sign Language (e.g., French influence on ASL, Milan Conference, A.G. Bell and Thomas Minor Gallaudet, and educational environments).
Relate information acquired in other subjects, such as the use of technology.
Students demonstrate an understanding of similarities and differences between the culture studied and the students’ own culture(s).
Compare and contrast social conventions of the target culture with the student(s)’ own culture (e.g., family dynamics, deaf child/deaf parents, deaf child/hearing parents).
Students develop a better understanding of the English language through a study of the target language.
Compare vocabulary usage and structural patterns of the target language with English (e.g., conjugation of verbs and time markers)
Use level-appropriate idiomatic expressions and slang in the target language (e.g., “loser”, “whatever”, train-gone-finish).
Students identify current events of the target culture.
Give information regarding major current events of the target culture.
Understand the impact of major current events on the target culture.
Students develop and apply target language skills and cultural knowledge beyond the classroom setting for recreational, educational, and occupational purposes.
Develop and apply target language skills and cultural knowledge beyond the classroom setting for recreational, educational, and occupational purposes.
Illustrate how the target language and culture studied are evident in and through the media, entertainment, and technology.
Locate and use resources in the target language, such as individuals and organizations accessible through the community.
I. Communication (CO)
The students read passages (edited and/or authentic) appropriate for Latin I.
Demonstrate knowledge of vocabulary, the basic inflection systems, and syntax appropriate to Latin.
Employ techniques to assist in reading comprehension.
Demonstrate reading comprehension of simple Latin passages.
Draw conclusions and make inferences from selections read.
The students comprehend spoken Latin phrases, quotations, and expressions as a part of the process for understanding written Latin.
Comprehend and follow oral and written instructions.
Respond to simple questions, statements, commands, or other stimuli.
The students provide accurate, written English translations.
Select the most appropriate meanings for words based on context.
The students write simple phrases and sentences in Latin as a part of the process for understanding written Latin.
Observe rules of grammar and syntax appropriate for Latin I.
Write from dictation, selections appropriate for Latin I.
The students read Latin passages aloud with proper intonation and rhythm.
Develop a sense of meaningful phrase grouping with appropriate voice inflection.
Recognize and reproduce Latin vowel, consonant, and diphthong sounds.
II. Cultural Perspectives, Practices, and Products (CU)
The students demonstrate an understanding of perspectives, practices, and products of the Greco-Roman culture.

Demonstrate an understanding of the ancient Romans based on reading selections and discussions regarding celebrations, family and social structures, food and eating habits, living conditions, entertainment practices, and the concept of time.
The students interpret cultural practices of the Romans.

Demonstrate an understanding of the role of pater familias, the client/patron relationship, religious practices such as the Vestal Virgins, the importance of proper attire, architectural features, art forms, and facts of ancient history and geography.
Identify figures of early Roman legends and the principal Greek and/or Roman deities and mythological heroes and the stories associated with them as reflections of Roman values.
III. Connections and Comparisons (CC)
The students reinforce and further the knowledge of other disciplines through the study of Latin.
Recognize common Latin roots and prefixes/suffixes and apply this knowledge to English vocabulary skills.
Identify words and terms studied in Latin in other disciplines (science, social science, language arts, math, etc.).
Recognize and use Roman numerals and the vocabulary associated with counting.
Understand some Latin phrases, abbreviations, and mottoes used in English.
Investigate the influence of Latin on various professional fields.
The students acquire information and recognize distinctive viewpoints via the study of Latin and the Greco-Roman civilization.
Identify similarities and differences in ancient Roman and contemporary culture.
Recognize and compare plots and themes of classical mythology in contemporary literature and art.
Compare the influence of Latin and the Greco-Roman culture on the customs of other cultures.
I. Communication (CO)
The students read and translate passages (edited and authentic) appropriate for Latin II.
Demonstrate expanded knowledge of vocabulary, grammar, and syntax appropriate to Latin II.
Employ techniques to assist in reading comprehension.
Demonstrate reading comprehension of adapted and authentic Latin passages.
Draw conclusions and make inferences from reading selections.
The students comprehend spoken Latin phrases, quotations, and expressions as a part of the process for understanding written Latin.
Comprehend and follow oral and written instructions.
Respond to more complex questions, statements, commands, or other stimuli.
The students write more complex phrases and sentences in Latin as a part of the process for understanding written Latin (using technology where appropriate).
Observe rules of grammar and syntax appropriate for Latin II.
Write from dictation selections appropriate for Latin II.
The students read Latin passages aloud with accurate pronunciation, proper intonation, and rhythm.
Develop a sense of meaningful phrase grouping with appropriate voice inflection.
Acquire fluency in pronouncing Latin.
II. Cultural Perspectives, Practices, and Products (CU)
The students demonstrate an understanding of perspectives, practices, and products of the Greco-Roman culture.
Demonstrate an understanding of the ancient Romans based on reading selections and discussions regarding Roman political systems and history.
Demonstrate an understanding of the ancient Romans based on reading selections and discussions regarding Roman daily life and culture.
The students demonstrate an understanding and make critical assessments of the contributions of Roman culture.
Locate places of Roman influence throughout Roman territories.
Continue to expand on knowledge of architectural styles, art forms, and artifacts of the Romans.
Increase their knowledge of classical mythology and legends.
III. Connections and Comparisons (CC)
The students reinforce and expand their knowledge of other disciplines and modern day classical influences through the study of Latin.
Recognize Latin roots and prefixes/suffixes and apply this knowledge to English vocabulary skills.
Identify words and terms studied in Latin with words in other disciplines.
Understand additional Latin phrases, abbreviations, and mottoes now used in  modern cultures.
Recognize Greco-Roman influences on community or state.
The students acquire information from reading passages and consequently recognize the distinctive viewpoints of Latin and the Greco-Roman civilization.
Identify similarities and differences in ancient and contemporary cultures.
Recognize and relate classical influences on modern social and political systems.
Demonstrate the influences of the Latin language and literature on English literature and artistic styles.
Initiate, sustain, and close oral and written exchanges in Spanish, applying increasingly accurate vocabulary and structures.
